(note this was changed because we already return data for story points completed by date but not time per story, and a standard deviation was too big of a difference in most cases and didn't seem to make sense)    # The projections must be displayed somewhere outside of the chart as well.  # Released versions do not need to show projections.  # Show the intended release date on the chart (midnight end of the date specified).  # Do not show calculations of velocity in the page.  # Take NWD into account with the calculation of mean and SD.  # Tooltips for potential targets show "type" (optimistic, pessimistic) and date.  # At least 10% of total estimated work for the Version must be completed before projections are shown.    (!) Development Notes (!)  Q: What if users are using negative story points?  A: If the predicted release date is in the past, we shouldn't display a prediction at all.    Q: What if any of the rates approach infinity?  A: We probably need to discuss with [~jrogers] [~mjopson] [~tkotecki] what is a good point to cut off. At the moment, if the predicted date is infinity, we don't display a prediction. If the most pessimistic date is infinity, we display a flat line and cut the chart off after the predicted date. But as long as it's not infinity, we display it.     Q: What distinguishes a rate that approaches vertical from the scope change events that ARE vertical?  A: (x) Not Answered (Don't really understand this question)    Q: What happens when users change the estimation of their board, or remove it completely?  A: The board will reflect the current estimation.     Q: What if the estimation uses time tracking? Will we calculate deviation in minutes, hours, days? How will this be reflected on the axis?  A: We will calculate units (of estimation) completed per day and use this to project. The axis won't change from what is displayed now: X is time and Y is estimation units.    Q: What if the user is tracking work with Logged Work? Should this rate also be displayed?  A: (x) Not Answered (do you mean time tracking?)    Q: What do we display if a projected line goes significantly into the future before it touches the scope line? Potentially this could be years into the future, but this isn't realistic to display.  A: The chart expands to display the most pessimistic date, unless it's infinity, in which case it expands to show the middle prediction. It could be years.     Q: What happens to the lines if the version has been released?  A: If the version is marked as released or if total scope = total completed scope then we shouldn't display a prediction.     Q: What happens to the lines if the "predicted" release date is moved to the past?  A: This should never happen.    Q: What happens if the release date is moved into the future?  A: Marking a version as released is separate from displaying a release date. Regardless of whether a release date is assigned, we will try to predict a date if total scope > completed scope and the version is not marked as released in JIRA.    Q: What if the release date is removed?  A: See above.    Q: What happens to the projected lines if issues are removed from the version (resulting in scope change) or deleted entirely (resulting in chart change without history)?  A: The scope line stays horizontal so if scope is removed then it moves down and in theory the release date should move forward. If the story was completed before it was removed then it's used in the calculation of average points completed per day.

story_text: Edit startDate of a fixVersion in JIRA: As Patrick, I want to be able to edit startDate for a fixVersion in JIRA so that the experience between GH and JIRA is consistent and not confusing.    h4. Approach for JIRA 6.0    JIRA code base:  * Modify JIRA's {{version}} table to include a start date column  * Make the above schema change on JIRA 5.2.x branch so that downgrades continue to work.  * Augment the RESTful table in JIRA's Project Admin page for Versions to show this start date and allow editing of it  * Augment the REST representation of versions to include start date  * Add the start date field to the JIRA API for a Version object    GH code base:  * Make GH aware of the capability of storing start date in either JIRA or GH, depending on the JIRA version.  * Migrate data stored in GH for start date to JIRA storage if available.    (!) Development Notes (!)    Q: How will adding a startDate to a previously released version affect the issues that apply to it?  A: (/) If a previously released version has a release date, the new start date will be validated against the release date.    Q: How will adding a startDate affect issues that have been Archived?  A: (/) Changes to version data do not affect issues.    Q: Which timezone setting will the startDate follow, user's or system's?  A: (/) Start date and release date are both stored in the JVM system time zone.    Q: Can you remove the startDate after adding it?  A: (/) Yes.    Q: What is one way to get the startDate to come after the releaseDate?  A: (/) Not possible.    Q: What happens when you add a startDate to a version that has been released but has no releaseDate?  A: (/) Start date can be added without release date.    Q: Is there a special place in the database this column/row will go? Depending on the database used, is there a way for this location to be already taken, and/or do we recycle a location/field ID? We need to protect against overwrites.  A: (/) The field {{startdate}} did not exist in the table {{projectversion}} before now.    Q: How is the data synchronised between GH AO tables and JIRA?  A: (/) When the Version meta data is asked for in GH, it will check to see if it exists in JIRA as well. If so, it will be migrated over.

*   **Example 1 (13 points):** "Horizontal swimlane... Based on Issue Priority Blocker - Only show swimlane when necessary - Warning if swimlane exceeds max amount - JQL longer term". This is a complex new UI component with intricate display logic and conditions. The wizard story is also a complex UI, but it's an entire *process* that replaces and integrates with existing core functionality, with a broader impact and more explicit edge cases.
*   **Example 2 (8 points):** "Version can be set in the create issue screen... not in GreenHopper." This involves adding a field and handling permissions, which is less complex than an entire multi-step flow replacing several dialogs.

The "Wizard-like Create Board dialog" story encompasses a broad range of complexities: major UI development, extensive refactoring/replacement of existing code, deep integration with multiple parts of the application, and thorough handling of numerous edge cases. Given the examples, especially the 13-point story which represents a significant feature, this wizard story appears to be at least on par with, if not more complex than, the 13-point example due to its re-engineering aspect, broad scope, and the sheer number of detailed requirements and edge cases outlined.

Estimated Story Points: 8

**Reasoning:**

This story, while appearing to be a simple banner display, has several layers of complexity that elevate it beyond a typical UI task.

1.  **Banner UI (Simple):** Creating the text banner and link itself is straightforward.
2.  **Global Display (Medium):** The banner needs to appear on *every* JIRA page, which requires integration into a global JIRA template or using a global message API.
3.  **Administrator-Specific (Easy):** Identifying if the current user is an administrator is a standard JIRA API call.
4.  **Persistent "Once Only" (Medium-High):** The banner should appear only *once* for *each* administrator. This implies storing a "seen" state persistently (e.g., in user preferences or a database flag) for each individual administrator user. This is more complex than a session-based flag.
5.  **Complex Conditional Logic (High):** This is the most significant factor. The banner should *not* be displayed if:
    *   The user installs GreenHopper for the first time and has **no boards**. This requires checking the existence of GreenHopper boards.
    *   **All** the existing boards are **already migrated**. This implies querying the migration status of potentially multiple boards, which could be a non-trivial database query or service call, and then aggregating that status to ensure *all* are migrated. This check needs to be performed efficiently.
6.  **Edge Cases (Easy):** Handling unlicensed GH, anonymous users, and stacking with other banners are relatively standard checks.

**Comparison with Examples:**

*   **Example 4 (2 points):** A simple link. Much less complex.
*   **Example 3 (3 points):** A UI component swap (autocomplete). Simpler than the conditional logic and persistence required here.
*   **Example 5 (5 points):** Showing subtasks and a default filter. This involves some UI display logic and configuration, but likely doesn't have the same level of persistent user state management or complex multi-entity conditional checks (like "all boards migrated") as the current story.
*   **Example 2 (8 points):** Version setting in create issue screen, permissions, and backlog management. This sounds like it involves deeper integration with JIRA's core permissions and possibly data model interactions. The current story's complex conditional display and persistence logic for the banner make it comparable in effort due to the database interactions and robust checks required.
*   **Example 1 (13 points):** A full-blown horizontal swimlane with multiple conditions, WIP limits, JQL integration, and Kanban concepts. Significantly more complex.

The combination of persistent per-user state, global display, and the detailed, multi-condition logic (especially checking the migration status of *all* boards) pushes this story well beyond 5 points and makes 8 points a reasonable estimate, similar to the depth of integration implied in Example 2.

Estimated Story Points: 8

**Reasoning:**

This story involves several layers of complexity that push it beyond a simple UI change:

1.  **Dynamic UI Logic (Front-end):**
    *   Detecting when quick filters "wrap to more than one line" requires responsive JavaScript logic that monitors screen width and the cumulative width of the filters. This can be tricky to implement robustly across different browsers and screen sizes.
    *   Conditionally rendering the "... Show more" link and hiding/showing filters.
    *   Toggling the link text ("... Show more" / "Show less").

2.  **State Management (Front-end):**
    *   Maintaining the current collapsed/expanded state within the UI.

3.  **User-level Persistence (Full-stack):**
    *   The requirement for the collapsed/expanded state to be "persistent on a user level" means this preference needs to be saved and retrieved for each user. This typically involves:
        *   A backend API endpoint to save the user's preference.
        *   A backend API endpoint to load the user's preference when the page loads.
        *   Potentially a database schema change or interaction with an existing user preference service.
        *   Front-end code to interact with these APIs.

4.  **Testing:**
    *   Thorough testing is needed for:
        *   Different screen widths and responsive behavior.
        *   Various numbers of quick filters.
        *   Cross-browser compatibility.
        *   User login/logout scenarios to ensure persistence works correctly.
        *   Interaction with existing features like header collapse.

**Comparison to Examples:**

*   **Example 1 (13 points - Swimlanes):** This is a very large feature, introducing new core UI elements and complex business logic. The current story is significantly less complex.
*   **Example 2 (8 points - Version in GreenHopper, Permissions):** This involves integrating an existing Jira field into GreenHopper, which can have its own data models, and also deals with permission-based logic. The complexity of the "user-level persistence" and dynamic UI for the current story feels comparable to this level of effort.
*   **Example 3 (3 points - Autocomplete update):** This is primarily a component swap/update. The current story involves more complex dynamic UI logic and persistence.
*   **Example 4 (2 points - Simple link):** This is a trivial UI change.
*   **Example 5 (5 points - Show subtasks, default filter):** This involves extending an existing view and adding a *default* (not user-persistent) filter. The current story's dynamic wrap detection and *user-level persistence* make it more complex than this.

The combination of dynamic UI behavior (detecting when elements wrap) and especially the "user-level persistence" that requires both front-end and backend work, pushes this story into the 8-point range.
